summary: True ribs (1-7) attach directly to the sternum, false ribs (8-10) attach indirectly, and floating ribs (11-12) have free anterior ends.
True ribs are 1-10 and floating ribs are 11-12.
All ribs attach directly to the sternum via costal cartilages.
The sternal angle (angle of Louis) is at the level of T4 and marks the level of the second rib and tracheal bifurcation.
The sternal angle is at the level of T2 and marks the first rib.
The sternal angle is the junction between the body and xiphoid process.
The intercostal neurovascular bundle runs in the order VAN (vein, artery, nerve) from superior to inferior in the costal groove.
The intercostal bundle runs in the order NAV from superior to inferior.
The intercostal bundle runs along the upper border of the rib.
The diaphragm is the primary muscle of inspiration and is supplied by the phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5).
The diaphragm is the primary muscle of expiration.
The diaphragm is supplied by the intercostal nerves.
The three diaphragmatic openings are the caval opening (T8), oesophageal hiatus (T10), and aortic hiatus (T12).
The aortic hiatus is at T8 and the caval opening is at T12.
All three diaphragmatic openings are at the level of T10.
In a flail chest, paradoxical chest wall movement occurs due to a free-floating segment from multiple adjacent rib fractures.
Flail chest involves fracture of a single rib.
Paradoxical chest movement is a normal finding during deep breathing.
A chest drain should be inserted just above the upper border of a rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle in the costal groove.
A chest drain should be inserted just below the lower border of a rib.
The position of chest drain insertion relative to ribs does not matter.

overallFeedback.feedback: Good effort! Revise the landmarks and clinical correlations.
